posts.name: Kay B Wolfe
posts.cooked:
Hi all. I’m new and was driven here by an obsession to figure out how to pronounce a wine I opened last weekend, Philip Togni. Absolutely obsessed with correct pronunciation, i turned to Google to help me figure out how to pronounce Togni. Unfortunately, the obsession worsened when Google revealed 2 different spellings of Mr. Togni’s name. Most people don’t even notice the subtle difference betw Togni and Tongi, but I do, and i’m unable to ignore it. So the question in summary is, Is it ToGNi with the G before the N or ToNGi with the N before the G?
Thanks to anyone who can help. I know it’s probs minor to many of you, but it’s driving me batty not to know. BTW, it was delicious. Just a stunningly beautiful wine.

K

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked: Edit: whoops

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
I always thought the G was silent so it was sort of pronounced Ton-yeh. Am I wrong? I visited them once and I should have asked! This was about a decade ago and Philip was I think approaching 80 but still out on his tractor…his daughter was a wonderful hostess.

Which vintage did you drink? Togni wines truly come into their own after about 20 years and stay on the plateau for quite a long time - 1992 is still drinking great

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Correct!

Was there two weeks ago, a decade almost to the day since my first visit.

Philip was born in Windsor in the UK, but is of Italian Swiss origin, hence the name.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Correct!

Was there two weeks ago, a decade almost to the day since my first visit.

so which is correct, silent G?

posts.name: Kay B Wolfe
posts.cooked: I drank a 2014 and she was drinking GREAT after a 1 hour decant. Guess I’ll hold off for another 10 years on the next bottle. Thanks.

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
KayW:

I drank a 2014 and she was drinking GREAT after a 1 hour decant. Guess I’ll hold off for another 10 years on the next bottle. Thanks.

I’m sitting on a few. These wines are built for the long haul.

Might need to hit the 2007

posts.name: John Preston
posts.cooked:
I have a couple birth year mags for our daughter…2001 vintage. Looking forward to uncorking those with her at some point…

Cheers,
JP

posts.name: Mike Silverman
posts.cooked: I had a 1996 last month. It was singing. Very elegant and hit a new note every few sips.

posts.name: jordan whitehead
posts.cooked:
PHILIP TOE KNEE

(complete sentence, blah blah blah)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Yes. The diphthong “gn” is pronounced like the “ñ” in Spanish.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Yes. The diphthong “gn” is pronounced like the “ñ” in Spanish.

got it, that’s how I’ve been pronouncing it - gold star.

posts.name: Kay B Wolfe
posts.cooked: exactly what I figured, but thanks for the confirmation!

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Yes. The diphthong “gn” is pronounced like the “ñ” in Spanish.

As delighted as I am to see the word diphthong used by someone other than me, I always thought it referred to a sound made from the combination of two vowels. Can it also be used for other gliding sounds?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Ah zut you’re right, it’s a digraph

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Ah zut you’re right, it’s a digraph

Glad that was cleared up quickly!

posts.name: Kay B Wolfe
posts.cooked: OMG, I KNEW that, but in my zeal for having my question answered, I blew it. Thank you for the clarification.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
brigcampbell:
William_Kelley:

Ah zut you’re right, it’s a digraph

Glad that was cleared up quickly!

It’s a huge relief to me.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Ah zut you’re right, it’s a digraph

All those years of speech and voice and dialect training pay off so rarely in my current life, I have to celebrate them where I can.

posts.name: Julian Davies
posts.cooked: So it’s tone-yee?
